items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: Is a tennis serve a closed or open skill?
items.correct: Closed - performed in a predictable, self-paced environment.
items.misconception: Open - because tennis is a fast-paced sport.
items.why: Students judge by the sport, not the moment. The serve happens at the server's pace with a stationary ball - closed. The return of serve is open. Skill classification depends on the SPECIFIC action's environment.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).
It's A buoyancy aid. “A hat only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Kayak turning over. “Going fast” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many river grades are there (1 to?)
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
